title: The Macro Minute: Why should investors care about Venezuela?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dell, to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
January 6, 2026. It's a blessing to be
back in front of you guys. Hope everyone
had a wonderful holiday season with
their families. Uh so, as always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. So, let's
dive right in. Today's Kacro question
is, why should investors care about
Venezuela? The short answer, if the
Trump administration is successful at
reviving Venezuela's energy industry, it
would represent a significant headwind
to reported inflation over the long term
via the persistence of structurally
depressed crude oil and gasoline prices.
Terms of analytical nuance, Venezuela
holds the largest proven crude oil
reserves in the world, estimated at
roughly 303 billion barrels. Those
reserves represent roughly 18% of total
glob and proven oil reserves. As of late
2025, Venezuela's crude oil production
is approximately 1 million barrels per
day, down 71% from the December 1997
peak when the country produced about
3.45 million barrels per day. US energy
equities including Chevron and Kle
Phillips extended gains as President
Trump outlined plans to rebuild
Venezuela's crude oil industry as the US
intensified pressure on now captured
Venezuelan President Nicholas Maduro's
hold on power in recent months. Oil
linked executives, legal advisers, and
investors increasingly lobbyed the Trump
administration and congressional staff
the bank deli Rodriguez Maduro's
long-erving second in command as his
successor. Ahead of Maduro's capture,
Rodriguez used meetings with financial
adviserss to scrutinize Venezuela's debt
profile and relationships with US oil
majors. Diligence that combined with the
absence of a long-standing US
indictment, position her as the
preferred leader among oil companies and
certain bond holders seeking to
restructure roughly $60 billion of
liabilities. Chevron stands to benefit
significantly from a successful
Venezuelan oil revival. Bakano Phillips
could also gain if it reenters the
country to recover approximately 10
billion in unpaid arbitration awards
from the 2007 asset nationalization.
Serving as oil minister, Rodriguez began
the became the primary liaison for
senior executives by steering
Venezuela's energy se sector through
sanctions, macroeconomic strain, and
[clears throat] operational dysfunction.
her track record as energy industry
advocates or track record has energy
industry advocates and lobbyists arguing
that she is uniquely positioned to
execute the USbacked restoration of
Venezuela's oil output. Sources familiar
with the matter said President Trump's
inner circle independently reached the
same conclusion, viewing the vice
president as a pragmatic bridge between
the government and private capital, who
could stabilize Venezuela's oil ccentric
economy and accelerate US commercial
engagement more effectively than
dissident leader Maria Cortina Machado.
Energy companies uh with Venezuelan
exposure are now pressing the Trump
administration to rapidly unwind
sanctions so Rodriguez can deliver
tangible results, warning that delays
carry material risk. By late December,
Venezuela had begun shutting oil wells
after exhausting storage capacity for
output stranded by sanctions and further
shutins would undermine economic
recovery in Rodriguez and weaken
Rodriguez political grip. As always,
members of our global investor community
can trust that our institutional grade
risk management overlays, kiss and Dr.
Mo, help our portfolios navigate these
emergent risks better than investors
attempting to manage risk without
systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone. Transition
to our 42 macro dashboard is always a
wrap- up with a question from our
community. This one's titled repo market
continuing stress. Says hi community and

gratitude to DD and his team for his
contin continuing work that gives me
reassurance to invest in these insecure
times. Your work is much appreciated.

it looks like the repo market stress is
not alleviated yet by the 40 billion
injected last month. What are your
thoughts on what might be the
implications for the S&P 500 and gold in
the short to medium term? Uh said it
reads, "My guess is chop correct for the
S&P 500 and up for gold." Uh here's a
link if anyone wants to follow
developments for this. uh have a
wonderful and productive new 2026 year
and everyone. Thank you. So, and then
thank you for for the kind feedback and
uh and have a wonderful uh productive
new year for you as well. So, uh let's
get into this. So, uh let me start by
showing a chart of the uh the Fed's
policy rates uh with sulfur being the
blue line there kind of in the middle of
those rates. The red line is the Fed
discount window/standy repo facility
rate. Uh the black line is the interest
on reserve balances. That's the
essentially the minimum rate that the
banks will participate in the repo
market at because obviously they they
can take uh get that risk-free yield
from the Fed as opposed to continuing to
push rates lower. You have the effective
Fed funds rate which is usually about 10
basis points below that. And then you
have the uh reverse repo facility which
is the minimum rate uh that uh um
non-bank market participants like uh
GSC's and and and money market funds
will participate in the repo market. And
so typically what you see in the repo
market is this a negative spread between
sofur and IB. And the reason that spread
is negative is because the Fed has been
targeting uh so-called ample reserves
regime whereby there's enough reserves
uh in the uh global financial sector in
this in this market such that the you
can have the rate unsecured overnight
financing be persistently below the rate
that the minimum rate that banks are
willing to pay because it ultimately
means that the GSC's and money market
fund participants the non-bank actors in
the repo market have enough cash
essentially to beat that rate below IRB
the interest on reserve balances uh
since uh the beginning of Q4, really
late Q3 when we started to observe uh
the stress in the funding markets and
really started to really highlight that
uh in early October uh when we saw the
spread start to trend higher uh trend to
a positive spread and persistently
positive spread. You typically see these
month end spikes uh in sofa IRB that's
that's pretty typical. Uh but what's
been unusual for the past kind of three
to four months is the fact that the
spread has persistently gone to uh one
high levels but most importantly has
refused to go back to this kind of
persistently negative condition which in
our you know our interpretation is that
it's an indication that there's a a lack
of ample reserves in the system a lack
of liquidity uh in the system uh and
ultimately this is why we saw the Fed uh
one stop QT or it's not they're not
doing QT they haven't been doing QT the
entire time they doing balance sheet
runoff very different than selling bonds
into the market, which they'll probably
never do. So, they stopped balance sheet
runoff and then they pivoted almost, you
know, shortly after that to uh this
reserve management operation uh
purchases authorizing $40 billion a
month in the month of December. Well,
very clearly we're, you know, 6 days
into January and we still have not
gotten the sofa IRB spread uh back below
uh zero, which would indicate there's an
abundance of liquidity uh in the in in
the in the financial sector. And so
ultimately it's our it's been our view
that the Federal Reserve is highly
likely to increase uh the quantity of
reserve management operation purchases
over the medium term in order to to to
to amilarate this sort of condition that
we've had essentially too low bank
reserves in the system that are floating
around the $13 trillion repo market. Um
so ultimately though we think that
they're going to do that. It may take
and then here's the the answer to the
question. It may take a correction in
risk assets andor a a widening of of
term premium and credit spreads. And
we're already seeing the we're already
seeing both of those actually in recent
weeks. A widening of term premium credit
spreads, albeit modestly, uh in order to
get the Fed's attention that hey, your
$40 billion a month in reserve
management operation purchases is not
enough. You need to do more. We
ultimately expect them to do whatever
quantity is required to get this spread
back below uh um back below zero on a
persistent basis which would indicate
that they've sort of solved the problem
of not having an ample amount of bank
reserves in the system. And one final
statistic I'll throw at you is that if
you look at bank reserves as a percent
of commercial bank assets as a proxy for
you know how high or low bank reserves
are, they're actually very very low. In
fact, I can just pull that chart up
before we wrap up. Let me pull that
chart up.
So if we look at
there we go. Yeah, here we go. Yeah. So
if we look at bank reserves as a
percentage of commercial bank assets,
it's somewhere around 12%. Yeah. In
fact, yeah, exactly. Is exactly at 12%.
That's the lowest number we've seen on a
on a durable basis since going back to
2018. And we all know what happened in
Q4 2018. So uh you know there's been so
many dynamics that are causing bank
reserves to be low. Obviously the
elevated uh TGA balance which is
currently at $781 billion that's about
30% of a commercial bank reserves. The
reason the TJ balance has been so
elevated in recent months is because
we've had this persistently dovish net
financing policy uh that was authored by
engineered uh or you know started by my
fellow Yelli Janet Yellen and after 18
months of lambasting our fellow Yelli uh
Treasury Secretary Scott Basset got in
the seat and essentially continued with
the doubbish net financing policy which
obviously reduces duration risk across
global financial markets from the
perspective of not supplying uh too many
coupons and bonds to investors. But the
the downside of this this non you know
all these policies are whenever you have
the a debt disease by the government
there's no real easy solution to deal
with it. This is one of the easier
solutions to deal with it in terms of
dovish net financing policy but it's not
a costless policy. Ultimately it
requires uh the TJ balance to be
elevated and that elevated TJ balance
has been contributo at the margins of of
of this low bank reserves condition that
we continue to observe. So ultimately
the key takeaway is that the Fed's going
to have to do more. Um whether they do
more on a proactive basis or on a
reactive basis as the tardy pal Fed has
historically been uh is the key question
that we need to answer as investors over
the next you know let's call it one to
three to maybe three to six months. I
don't know the answer to that. I don't
think anybody knows the answer to that
but I think we all know where this is
going to wind up which is more ARMP more
bank reserves higher markets and and
that's the kind of the key takeaway. So,
um, you know, if you're a trader, good
luck. If you're an investor and you you

and understand where this is all headed.
